item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to like this one, but there's a lot of emphasis put on how Nike was able to develop an anomalous golf ball instead of the anomalous golf ball itself. In addition, it's also implied that Nike was able to "take out" an SCP agent, without repercussion? I'm sure that, if a CIA agent disappeared in a Nike facility, they'd be investigated so hard, you wouldn't even be able to say "Nike" in public anymore. Some other bits of this (e.g. it somehow killing a researcher) aren't too good as well.

That doesn't mean this SCP is bad. There are quite a few good lines, and the implication that the SCP Foundation probably investigates abnormal sporting events is a great one (did they investigate the five minute mile?). However, it could definitely use improvement.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, it's Gungnir in golf ball form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here are a few things that made me downvote this SCP:

+ show block
– hide block

…when American golfer Tiger Woods scored…

The name should be redacted or put in black boxes. No additional horror can be gained from using real-world figures in this article.

…and Dr. Kaine's upper left torso.

This is just being violent for the sake of being violent. Gratuitous deaths and gores that could be avoided in-universe should not be in testing results. What, the Foundation forgot to take precautions and Dr. Kaine just happened to be in the wrong place? Come on.

The following is a compilation of all notable experiences of Foundation personnel….

Should be "findings" or something like that.

The only thing that is interesting from this article is the low white noise coming constantly from the ball and the loud and bright "explosion"1 that caught Agent ██. Other than that, it lacks a bit clinical tone and horror. It doesn't do anything for me.

Footnotes
1. But didn't anyone notice that? You should probably put in a procedure of amnestics to the staffs at the headquarters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what do you mean? for the page/number?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How much do yall want for this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the constructive criticism and the upvote!

In regards to your first nitpick, the doctor who was experimenting with the object was not killed, it was another doctor who was in the trajectory. I included that part for a little humor and to show the SCP could pass through organic material. I like your work-around on having it simply injure him though, so I might use that because I have been getting mixed responses on that part.

I also agree with you on the second part. Maybe I should leave out the part about an unidentifiable language. I still have some work to do with this SCP, so thanks for the criticism, I appreciate it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm… I like this, and +1!

I like that you didn't blank out the names of Nike and Tiger Woods. This wouldn't have the same amount of intrigue if it wasn't directly connected to real world figures.

I also like that this is a low-key SCP. It could belong on the list of Anomalous Objects but it's got the story behind it that makes for a good skip. It's interesting and intriguing, but the effect doesn't bend your brain and there aren't yards of logs to read through. It's like SCP popcorn.

Now for nitpicks!

I actually don't think that killing off the doctor who was experimenting with the object is a good decision? The reason this article stands out is its low profile, and killing two people off ruins the mood a bit. People die in practically every SCP test log; just having the guy be severely injured would be enough. Maybe it takes out his eye instead of killing him?

I like what's behind the door, but the idea of an agent opening a door marked in an "unidentifiable language" and then never being heard from again is… unsatisfying. I don't want to say cliched, but I do think there could be better options for relating the story of how Nike, a bajillion dollar corporation, got their hands on this thing or manufactured it and how it got on Woods' tee.

Overall, good skip. Thanks for writing it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right, fixed. Thanks :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpicky thing that I will leave up to the author to leave or correct:
"Inc." should be capitalized, since you are including it as part of the name of the company.

Not a huge deal, but since it shows up a number of times, it stood out to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a lot for coming back and taking a look. Which part of the revisions did you like? Was it the removal of Event-C?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With the recent revisions, I would like to change my +1 to +2. Since that is not an option, huzzah for the revisions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Appreciate it. I was considering redacting the name of the golfer, but I felt like having it correlate with his sudden lack of success starting in around 2013 would be a pretty interesting plotline. I am still in the process of messing around with the skip, though, so I might very well redact his name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey man. I have no idea about the terminology of decibels as well. I posted this skip in the IRC for review, and someone told me to update it from dB to dBa. Also from some quick research I just did, it seems that dBa, dBA, and dB(a) are all acceptable forms of writing decibels with an A-weighted filter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a simple SCP. Not overly interesting but not uninteresting.

The only thing I can really suggest is that you could maybe redact the name of the golfer who was found to be using it. It's probably fine how it is though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not an audio person, so this may be a stupid question, but what is "dBa"? Is it meant to be dB(A) (i.e. decibels with the A-weighting filter), or is it something else? Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. Will be adding some more to the article to explain a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a lot for the feedback. Looks like a mod/admin removed the tags so that's done. Also I will be adding a little bit extra to flesh it out, but I agree with you: I want to keep the original spirit of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removed one of them which i also agree was pretty unneccesary. The second one, I like though, because it gives the reader a tiny bit of imagination in an otherwise straightforward skip. Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My own headcanon suggests that while Mr. Woods might have made the request to the appropriate source, the actual anomaly was provided by quite another entity.

E.g., if TotleighSoft tried to make a superior golf ball, this is about what you'd get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"FORE!"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only real issues are the two [REDACTED]s. There's not much of a reason to hide the first, and I'm confused as to why you'd redact the second thing.

In response to the issue people are having with 'Nike straight-up has anomalous shit laying around', you could maybe rework a couple lines so that TW is given the item from someone claiming to be from Nike after they refuse his initial request for being insane/impossible/what have you.

You also might want to reword the SCProcedures. The way its worded makes it seem like its capable of teleporting or demanifesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. For this being a first article, you've shown quite some discipline. There are a lot of tempting spots to deviate, but you keep what's here short, relevant to the SCP, and believable (to me at least). If you are serious about adding to the back story, try to keep the spirit and flow of what's already here.

Also, you may want to remove the tags you added. There are rules for tags, and there are taggers on the site that are willing tag for you, but they won't see that the page needs tagging unless you leave the tag section completely blank.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Golf isn't a tag, you should delete your tags and wait for someone else to tag it.

Thank you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd say no to the log. Some hint as to what the hell is going on in the Nike product development offices would be a better direction to take this in, seconding Scorpion451 below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like this, but there's one pretty major aspect that keeps me from upvoting.

Normally I'm not against referencing real-world celebrities and companies, but in this case it kind of kills the article for me. It makes it sort of a "just so" story: "Nike made it for Tiger Woods" doesn't tell us much about where the heck this thing came from and how it was made, and actually would be a good argument for classifying Nike as a GOI.

I think the solution is related to minmin's critique above: Rather than "Nike made it for Tiger Woods", how and why else might this ball have been created, and how did it end up on Tiger Wood's tee?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: :) thanks for the feedback. I will think of some ways I can add direction to this. Maybe an experiment log? Or more backstory?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There isn't much of a direction to this, or escalation, or whatnot. It's just a thing. But there's a certain flavour you've packed around it that makes this article feel… comfortable, for lack of a better word. And I guess I can dig that.

item.title: Criticism wanted
item.content:encoded: Hello all, this is my first SCP and I plan on writing more in the future. Any criticism will be greatly appreciated. I already got a lot of feedback on the IRC channel from lots of helpful and kind people, but I will also read every comment on here!
